infection.title: This needs medical attention today
infection.body: What you've described — fever, red streaks, or a hot painful breast — are signs that may need treatment today, not tomorrow. Mastitis and breast abscesses can progress quickly.

actions.label: Call your doctor or midwife now
actions.detail: Describe your symptoms exactly. Ask about antibiotics if you have fever + a hard, hot, red area.

actions.label: If you can't reach them, go to urgent care
actions.detail: Don't wait to see if it improves on its own when fever is involved.

actions.label: Keep feeding or pumping if you can
actions.detail: Stopping milk removal makes engorgement and infection worse. Breastfeeding with mastitis is safe for your baby.

infection.reassurance: Getting treatment doesn't mean you have to stop breastfeeding. Most mothers continue successfully after treatment.
infection.disclaimer: This is not a diagnosis. Only a healthcare provider can evaluate and treat a breast infection.

bleeding.title: Bleeding needs to be checked
bleeding.body: Cracked nipples that bleed are painful but usually manageable. However, heavy bleeding, blood in your milk, or a wound that won't heal needs a professional to look at it.

actions.label: Contact a lactation consultant (IBCLC)
actions.detail: Cracked and bleeding nipples are almost always a latch problem. An IBCLC can fix the latch in one session and stop the damage.

actions.label: Call your doctor if the wound looks open or infected
actions.detail: Signs: yellow or green discharge, smell, skin pulling apart, increasing pain after day 5.

bleeding.reassurance: Bleeding nipples are one of the most common reasons mothers stop breastfeeding — and one of the most fixable with the right help.
bleeding.disclaimer: This is not a diagnosis. A healthcare provider or IBCLC should assess any bleeding that concerns you.

mental_health.title: Please reach out right now
mental_health.body: What you're feeling is real, and you deserve support immediately. Thoughts of harming yourself or your baby are a medical emergency — not a sign that you're a bad mother.

actions.label: Call or text a crisis line now
Tell someone you trust right now
actions.detail: Your partner, mother, a friend — anyone. Don't be alone with these thoughts.

actions.label: Call your doctor or go to the nearest hospital
actions.detail: Tell them exactly what you told us. Postpartum mental health crises are treated and recoverable.

mental_health.reassurance: 1 in 5 mothers experience postpartum depression or anxiety. You are not alone, and this is not your fault.
mental_health.disclaimer: If you are in immediate danger, call emergency services (112 in India, 911 in US/Canada).

baby_emergency.title: Your baby needs emergency care now
baby_emergency.body: The signs you've described in your baby need immediate medical evaluation. Do not wait.

actions.label: Call emergency services or go to the nearest hospital now
actions.detail: 112 in India · 911 in US/Canada · 999 in UK. Tell them your baby's age and the symptoms.

baby_emergency.disclaimer: Do not delay emergency care for a newborn or infant showing signs of distress.

default.title: This sounds like it needs a professional
default.body: What you've described is outside what we can safely guide you through here. Please contact your doctor, midwife, or a certified lactation consultant (IBCLC) today.

actions.label: Call your doctor or midwife
actions.detail: Describe your symptoms exactly as you described them here.

default.reassurance: Asking for help is the right call. This app is for guidance — not a replacement for care when you need it.
default.disclaimer: This is not a diagnosis. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider for medical concerns.
